{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.7,

    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.1",
    "R1.2",
    "R1.3",
    "R1.5",
    "R1.6",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "To scope “Improve this draft,” should I: A) perform a light copyedit focused on sentence-level clarity and consistency without changing structure or claims, or B) perform a substantive revision that also reframes the contribution, reorganizes sections, and checks logic and claim–evidence alignment (and may suggest citation fixes)? Please choose A or B.",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "The draft would benefit from a sharper framing of the core contribution and problem setting beyond sentence-level edits.",
    "R1.2": "The causal identification assumptions and ATE interpretation must be checked for internal logical coherence.",
    "R1.3": "The reported ATE, confidence interval, sensitivity bound, and subgroup effects should be verified to directly support the stated claims.",
    "R1.5": "Reordering and tightening the section-level flow (setup, data, method, identification, results, heterogeneity, limitations, contribution) can improve readability.",
    "R1.6": "Line-level clarity, concision, and tone can be improved without altering technical content.",
    "RX.1": "Any improvement should clearly communicate assumptions, uncertainty, and sensitivity limits.",
    "RX.2": "Edits must avoid overstating causal or generalization claims beyond what the identification and data support.",
    "RX.3": "Work should stay within draft improvement and not introduce new analyses or unverifiable content.",
    "RX.4": "Care is needed to avoid wording that could lead to harmful misinterpretation in product or policy decisions.",
    "RX.5": "All original numbers, methods, and attributions should be preserved and any substantive changes traceable."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: To scope “Improve this draft,” should I: A) perform a light copyedit focused on sentence-level clarity and consistency without changing structure or claims, or B) perform a substantive revision that also reframes the contribution, reorganizes sections, and checks logic and claim–evidence alignment (and may suggest citation fixes)? Please choose A or B.

rationale.R1.1: The draft would benefit from a sharper framing of the core contribution and problem setting beyond sentence-level edits.
rationale.R1.2: The causal identification assumptions and ATE interpretation must be checked for internal logical coherence.
rationale.R1.3: The reported ATE, confidence interval, sensitivity bound, and subgroup effects should be verified to directly support the stated claims.
rationale.R1.5: Reordering and tightening the section-level flow (setup, data, method, identification, results, heterogeneity, limitations, contribution) can improve readability.
rationale.R1.6: Line-level clarity, concision, and tone can be improved without altering technical content.
rationale.RX.1: Any improvement should clearly communicate assumptions, uncertainty, and sensitivity limits.
rationale.RX.2: Edits must avoid overstating causal or generalization claims beyond what the identification and data support.
rationale.RX.3: Work should stay within draft improvement and not introduce new analyses or unverifiable content.
rationale.RX.4: Care is needed to avoid wording that could lead to harmful misinterpretation in product or policy decisions.
rationale.RX.5: All original numbers, methods, and attributions should be preserved and any substantive changes traceable.
